summary: Joel Spolsky announces his new monthly column 'How Hard Could It Be?' in Inc. Magazine, focused on the business of software startups. He teases the first installment with a humorous anecdote about the absurdity of trying to print signatures onto already-printed forms, illustrating the kind of mundane operational headaches entrepreneurs face. The post is primarily a brief announcement rather than a substantive essay, serving to direct readers to the full column in Inc. Magazine.
key_points: Joel launched a monthly Inc. Magazine column called 'How Hard Could It Be?' about the business side of software startups
The teaser anecdote illustrates how seemingly simple office tasks (like signing forms) can spiral into frustrating technical problems
Joel positions himself as both a practitioner and student of entrepreneurship, noting he's learned a lot from Inc. Magazine over the years
money_quotes: Once a sheet of paper has been through a laser printer, the next time you try to print on it, it's going to fight back, jamming the printer and resulting in the deaths of three other pages, and you're going to spend five minutes with a putty knife cleaning up the bloodshed.
I didn't relish the carpal tunnel syndrome that would result from signing all these forms.
key_insight: Even the most mundane operational tasks in running a startup — like signing paperwork — can become unexpectedly complicated misadventures.
